interpretations.interpretation: The audience could understand that the suffering of the “one” described is not meaningless: it is portrayed as something God ‘delights’ in—meaning God permits or purposes painful treatment for a larger outcome (often read as part of a divine plan rather than random tragedy).

Many listeners would likely hear sacrificial/atonement language: if God ‘sets guilt/אָשָׁם’ on the person, then the suffering functions like a remedy for wrongdoing—i.e., guilt is dealt with through that person’s ordeal, similar in spirit to sacrificial rites known in Israelite worship.

The line about ‘seeing seed’ and ‘prolonging days’ would be taken as a promise of vindication after suffering: the wrong that occurs to the sufferer will not be the final word, and the person’s future (family/offspring and extended life) signals restoration and success.

Some audiences may interpret the “suffering servant” as a representative figure for the nation of Israel (or a righteous subset within it). In that reading, the nation’s affliction is framed as God’s instrument for accomplishing a salvific purpose, culminating in God’s pleasure and success ‘in the hand’ of the one who bears the burden.

The phrase ‘the will of YHWH is in his hand/prosperity’ could be read by contemporary hearers as empowerment and deliverance: God’s favor will ensure that what happens through the sufferer leads to real, observable success rather than defeat.

The audience might also construe the statement in moral-spiritual terms: even if the person suffers, it is not portrayed as God rejecting them; rather, divine purpose and right outcomes are affirmed, encouraging trust that God’s justice and mercy will prevail.
